content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 14, 1991

Honorable Diane E. Watson
Senator, 28th District
California State Senate
State Capitol, Room 4040
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-742

Dear Senator Watson:

     You have requested advice under the new ethics provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/  Because your request is a
general inquiry, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

QUESTIONS

     1.  If a Member of the Legislature joins a speakers' bureau
as an independent contractor, may he or she be paid for speaking
on nonlegislative topics?

     2.  If not, what, if any, condition or restriction may make
such an arrangement possible?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Section 89502 of the Ethics in Government Act of 1990
prohibits elected state officers from accepting an honorarium for
any speech given, regardless of the content of the speech.  The
statute creates an exception to the honorarium ban for income

_________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-742
Page 2


earned for a speech given by a state officer in connection with
the practice of a bona fide business, trade or profession.
However, this exception does not apply when the sole or
predominant activity of the Member's business, trade or profession
is making speeches. Income earned from the practice of making
speeches, such as a speakers' panel, is deemed to be an
honorarium. Thus, if a Member of the Legislature joins a
speakers' bureau, he or she is prohibited from accepting payment
for a speech given.

     2. A Member of the Legislature is not precluded from joining
a speakers' bureau and receiving payment for making speeches in
connection therewith if the Member does not use the honorarium,
and within 30 days the Member either returns the honorarium to the
donor or donates the honorarium to the State's General Fund
without taking a deduction for tax purposes.

ANALYSIS

     Section 89502 of the Ethics in Government Act of 1990
provides, in pertinent part, as follows:

          (a) No elected state officer may accept an
     honorarium.

          (b) "Honorarium" means, except as provided in
     subdivision (c), any payment made in consideration
     for any speech given, article published, or
     attendance at any public or private conference,
     convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
     gathering.

          (c) "Honorarium" does not include earned
     income for personal services which are customarily
     provided in connection with the practice of a bona
     fide business, trade, or profession, such as
     teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real
     estate, banking, or building contracting, unless
     the sole or predominant activity of the business,
     trade, or profession is making speeches. The
     commission shall adopt regulations to implement
     this subdivision.

          (d) Section 89506 shall apply to all
     payments, advances, and reimbursements for travel
     and related lodging and subsistence.

          (e) This section does not apply to any
     honorarium which is not used and, within 30 days
     after receipt, is either returned to the donor or

Our File No. I-90-742
Page 3


delivered to the Controller for donation to the
General Fund, without being claimed as a deduction
from income tax purposes.

Section 89502 (a)-(e),(emphasis
added).

The former statute relating to the limitations on gifts and
honorarium, Section 85400, had placed a $1000 limit on any speech
relating to the governmental process. This statute was repealed
and replaced by Sections 89501 - 89506.3/ Under the current
statute, state elected officers are prohibited from accepting an
honorarium for any speech given, regardless of the content of the
speech.

The statute creates an exception to the honorarium ban for
income earned for a speech given by a state officer in connection
with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession.
However, this exception does not apply when the sole or
predominant activity of the Member's business, trade or profession
is making speeches. Income earned from the practice of making
speeches, such as a speakers' bureau, is deemed to be honorarium.
Thus, in answer to your first question, if a Member of the
Legislature joins a speakers' bureau, he or she is prohibited from
accepting payment for a speech given.

You also asked whether there was any condition or restriction
which would make joining a speakers' bureau permissible. Clearly,
a Member of the Legislature is not precluded from joining a
speakers' bureau and receiving payment for making speeches in
connection therewith under the following conditions:

(1) The Member does not use the honorarium;

(2) Within 30 days the Member either returns the honorarium
to the donor or donates the honorarium to the State's General Fund
in accordance with subdivision (e) above.

The proviso is that the Member does not claim the honorarium as a
deduction from income for tax purposes. In this event, the
honorarium has no value to the state officer and need not be
disclosed.


3/ Section 89501: limitations on gifts and honorarium for local
elected officeholders. Section 89502: limitations on honorarium
for elected state officers. Section 89503: limitations on
honorarium for members of a state board or commission and for
designated employees of a state agency. Section 89504:
limitations on gifts for elected state officers. Section 89505:
limitations on gifts for members of a state board or commission
and for designated employees of a state agency. Section 89506:
limitations on payments, advances or reimbursements for travel.

Our File No. I-90-742
Page 4


I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[Signature: Deanne Stone]

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
